name: Hargrove v. State
text:
Syllabus. The case concerns the regulation of expressive conduct.

JUSTICE BRENNAN delivered the opinion of the Court. This case requires us to consider the First Amendment protection afforded to symbolic expression. Freedom of speech lies at the heart of our constitutional order. The State's restriction on expression cannot survive strict scrutiny because it targets the communicative impact of the message. Robust debate and free exercise of expressive liberty must be safeguarded. We hold the statute unconstitutional under the First Amendment. Reversed.

JUSTICE SCALIA, dissenting. The majority distorts the original meaning of the First Amendment. The text and historical understanding permit the State to regulate conduct, not merely speech. The framers did not contemplate that every expressive gesture would receive constitutional immunity. I would defer to the legislature and affirm. I respectfully dissent.

name: United States v. Coleman
text:
JUSTICE GINSBURG delivered the opinion of the Court. The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable search and seizure. Here the officers conducted a warrantless search lacking probable cause. The exclusionary rule requires suppression of the evidence so obtained. Individual privacy against arbitrary government intrusion is a core guarantee. We reverse the conviction and remand.

JUSTICE ALITO, with whom JUSTICE THOMAS joins, dissenting. The good-faith exception should govern. The officers reasonably relied on exigent circumstances, and suppression imposes an intolerable cost on law enforcement. The majority's rigid rule ignores the practical realities of policing. I would affirm. I respectfully dissent.

name: Meyer v. Commissioner
text:
JUSTICE SCALIA delivered the opinion of the Court. This dispute involves economic regulation and the proper scope of agency authority over interstate commerce. The statute's text controls. An administrative agency may not exceed the regulatory power Congress conferred. The taking alleged here does not rise to a compensable level under the Takings Clause. We affirm the agency's determination.

JUSTICE BRENNAN, dissenting. The Court reads the regulatory statute too narrowly. Congress intended broad protection against economic exploitation, and the agency's expansive interpretation deserves deference. The majority's cramped textualism undermines the remedial purpose of the regulation. I dissent.

name: Doe v. Board of Education
text:
JUSTICE MARSHALL delivered the opinion of the Court. The Equal Protection Clause forbids invidious racial classification. The Board's policy draws a suspect classification and must satisfy strict scrutiny. Because the policy perpetuates discrimination without a compelling justification, it cannot stand. Equal dignity under law demands no less. Reversed.

JUSTICE BRENNAN, concurring. I join the Court's opinion in full. I write separately to emphasize that the guarantee of equal protection extends robustly to remedy historical discrimination and to vindicate civil rights.

JUSTICE SCALIA, dissenting. The Constitution is colorblind in a different sense than the majority supposes. The text does not authorize the judiciary to second-guess every legislative classification. I would apply rational-basis review and affirm. I respectfully dissent.

name: State of Texas v. United States
text:
JUSTICE SCALIA delivered the opinion of the Court. This case implicates federalism and the limits of federal power under the Commerce Clause. The Tenth Amendment reserves to the States a sphere of sovereignty the federal government may not invade. The anti-commandeering principle forbids Congress from conscripting state officials. The original understanding of state sovereignty controls. We hold the federal statute exceeds the commerce power. Reversed.

JUSTICE GINSBURG, with whom JUSTICE BREYER joins, dissenting. The majority revives a cramped vision of federal authority. The Commerce Clause grants Congress ample power to address national problems, and the statute is a valid exercise of that power. The Court's federalism doctrine substitutes judicial preference for constitutional text. I respectfully dissent.
